title: Part 1: Topic Selection — immigration as APUSH research subject
html:
Part 1: Topic Selection — immigration as APUSH research subject

Source: 2025-01-23 Google Doc — appears to be an APUSH (or APUSH-equivalent) Part-1 topic-selection assignment. Yahir picks immigration (1840–1914) and connects it to his identity as a first-generation Mexican-American. NOTE: The 2025 date is the file-sync date; the content reads as a much earlier high-school assignment (likely 2020–2021 Grants Pass APUSH course), re-surfaced.

TL;DR

  • Topic chosen — immigration (1840–1914), framed as "one of the most contentious and complex issues in American society today."
  • Personal-stake declaration — "I am a first generation American, as my parents immigrated here from Mexico" — explicit identity-claim that connects historical study to lived family arc.
  • Historical scope — Irish potato famine, Ellis Island (opened 1892), Chinese railroad workers, Chinese Exclusion Act (1882), tenement conditions, factory labor.
  • Five research questions — industrial development, anti-immigrant sentiment, cultural identity preservation, immigrant labor organizations, immigration-policy legacy.
  • Current-events tie-in — US-Mexico border debate, sanctuary cities, skilled-tech immigration, asylum-seeker detention conditions — all framed as echoes of 1840–1914 patterns.

Pattern observed

This is the earliest cataloged document where Yahir explicitly names immigration lawyer as a future direction — actually that articulation appears in the 2023 Lewis & Clark essay, not here, but the same identity-frame (first gen Mexican American family lineage → academic interest in immigration → eventual professional commitment) is the through-line. This APUSH note is the academic-research expression of the same family-arc that produces the L&C essay and connects to Mom / Dad's Mexico origin story.

The 1882 Chinese Exclusion Act mention rhymes weirdly with Yahir's own 2024 China chapter visa paperwork — the historical-policy lens he applied as a teenager prefigures his lived 2024 experience as the inverse case (American student getting a Chinese visa via CIEE).

Open questions

  • Confirm the actual class — APUSH? OSU history elective? The doc structure (numbered items 1–5, "Topic Selection" header) reads as high-school AP, but the file-sync date is January 2025.
  • Did the assignment continue to Parts 2/3? (No corresponding "Part 2" doc surfaces in the cataloged corpus.)
